md: Right-triangle trig only works for angles between $0¬į$ and $90¬į$ ‚ÄĒ that's all the right triangle has room for. But angles can be bigger than $90¬į$. They can be negative. They can keep going past $360¬į$. To handle every angle, we need a richer picture: the **unit circle**.
### Angle in standard position
An angle is in **standard position** when:
**1. Its vertex is at the origin** $(0, 0)$.
**2. Its initial side lies along the positive x-axis.**
Then we rotate the **terminal side** away from the initial side. The amount we rotate is the angle.
**Positive angles** rotate counterclockwise (from +x toward +y). **Negative angles** rotate clockwise (from +x toward -y). So $60¬į$ goes up-right, $-60¬į$ goes down-right.

blocks.title: Naming the quadrant
blocks.body: An angle's *quadrant* is wherever its terminal side ends up. $135¬į$ ‚Üí terminal side in **Quadrant II**. $250¬į$ ‚Üí **Quadrant III**. Angles whose terminal side falls *on* an axis (e.g., $0¬į, 90¬į, 180¬į, 270¬į$) are called **quadrantal** angles.

md: ### What is the unit circle?
The circle of radius $1$ centered at the origin:
Pick an angle $\theta$ in standard position. The terminal side hits the unit circle at exactly one point. That point is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$.
**That's the definition.** For *any* angle ‚ÄĒ small, big, negative, doesn't matter ‚ÄĒ $\cos\theta$ is the x-coordinate of that point and $\sin\theta$ is the y-coordinate.
Drag the blue point around the circle (or use the slider). Watch the cos and sin readouts change as you cross quadrants.
### Why this matches right-triangle trig
For an angle between $0¬į$ and $90¬į$, the radius and its x/y projections form a right triangle inside the circle. The hypotenuse is $1$, so:
Same definitions, expressed using coordinates. The unit circle just generalizes them to *any* angle.
### Coterminal angles
Two angles are **coterminal** if they share the same terminal side ‚ÄĒ they end up at the same point on the unit circle (and so have the same sin, cos, tan).
Add or subtract a full revolution and you land back where you started:
**Example.** $400¬į$ is coterminal with $400¬į - 360¬į = 40¬į$. They have the same sin/cos/tan.
**Example.** $-50¬į$ is coterminal with $-50¬į + 360¬į = 310¬į$.
**Tip.** To find the coterminal angle in $[0¬į, 360¬į)$, keep adding or subtracting $360¬į$ until you land in that range.
### The four quadrants
As $\theta$ rotates around the circle, $(x, y)$ visits each quadrant. The signs of cos and sin tell you where:
**Quadrant I** ($0¬į$‚Äď$90¬į$): $\cos > 0$, $\sin > 0$
**Quadrant II** ($90¬į$‚Äď$180¬į$): $\cos < 0$, $\sin > 0$
**Quadrant III** ($180¬į$‚Äď$270¬į$): $\cos < 0$, $\sin < 0$
### ASTC ‚ÄĒ All Students Take Calculus
A mnemonic for which trig functions are *positive* in each quadrant:
**Q I** ‚ÄĒ **A**ll three (sin, cos, tan) are positive.
**Q II** ‚ÄĒ only **S**ine is positive.
**Q III** ‚ÄĒ only **T**angent is positive.
**Q IV** ‚ÄĒ only **C**osine is positive.
Read it counterclockwise: A ‚Üí S ‚Üí T ‚Üí C, starting from Q I.

blocks.title: Why ASTC works
blocks.body: $\sin = y$, $\cos = x$, $\tan = y/x$. In Q II, $x < 0$ and $y > 0$ ‚Üí cos negative, sin positive, tan negative. In Q III, both $x, y < 0$ ‚Üí cos and sin negative, but tan = (-)/(-) = positive. Each quadrant follows from the signs of $x$ and $y$.

md: ### Reference angles

A **reference angle** is the *acute* angle (always $0¬į \le \alpha \le 90¬į$) that the terminal side makes with the **nearest x-axis**. Reference angles let you reduce *any* angle problem to a $0¬į$‚Äď$90¬į$ problem you already know how to solve.
**How to find it:** look at the quadrant of $\theta$, then:
**Q I:** $\alpha = \theta$
**Q II:** $\alpha = 180¬į - \theta$
**Q III:** $\alpha = \theta - 180¬į$
**Q IV:** $\alpha = 360¬į - \theta$
(In radians, replace $180¬į$ with $\pi$ and $360¬į$ with $2\pi$.)
**Example.** Reference angle of $210¬į$? Q III, so $\alpha = 210¬į - 180¬į = 30¬į$.
**Example.** Reference angle of $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$? Q II, so $\alpha = \pi - \dfrac{5\pi}{6} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.
### Special angles you should know
These show up everywhere. Each gives clean exact values:
### Finding the exact value of trig ratios at any angle
Combine **reference angle** + **ASTC** and you can evaluate sin, cos, or tan at *any* angle on the unit circle. Three steps:
**Step 1.** Identify the quadrant of $\theta$.
**Step 2.** Find the reference angle $\alpha$.
**Step 3.** Look up the value of the trig function at $\alpha$ (using the special-angle table). Then attach the sign from ASTC.
**Example.** Find $\cos 210¬į$.
‚Üí Quadrant III. Reference angle: $210¬į - 180¬į = 30¬į$.
‚Üí $\cos 30¬į = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. In Q III, cosine is *negative* (only T is positive). So $\cos 210¬į = -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
**Example.** Find $\sin 315¬į$.
‚Üí Q IV. Reference angle: $360¬į - 315¬į = 45¬į$.
‚Üí $\sin 45¬į = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. In Q IV, sine is negative. So $\sin 315¬į = -\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
### Trig ratios at angles in radians
Everything above works the same in radians ‚ÄĒ you just use $\pi$ instead of $180¬į$. The unit-circle definitions don't depend on units.
**Common radian angles and their special values:**
**Example in radians.** Find $\sin\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$.
‚Üí Quadrant III ($\pi < \tfrac{4\pi}{3} < \tfrac{3\pi}{2}$). Reference angle: $\tfrac{4\pi}{3} - \pi = \tfrac{\pi}{3}$.
‚Üí $\sin\tfrac{\pi}{3} = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. In Q III, sine is negative.
‚Üí $\sin\dfrac{4\pi}{3} = -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
### Tangent on the unit circle
Tangent is the slope of the radius ‚ÄĒ the rise (sin) over the run (cos):
When $\cos\theta = 0$ (at $90¬į$ and $270¬į$), tan is **undefined** ‚ÄĒ the radius is vertical, slope is undefined. Try the widget at $\theta = 90¬į$ and watch the tan readout.
### The Pythagorean identity
Every point $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ is on the unit circle, so it satisfies $x^2 + y^2 = 1$:
True for *every* angle. This is the most fundamental identity in all of trigonometry ‚ÄĒ the Pythagorean theorem in disguise.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Standard position ‚Üí angle's terminal side determines the quadrant. Coterminal angles share a terminal side ($\theta \pm 360¬į$). Reference angle is the acute angle to the nearest x-axis. To find any trig value: quadrant + reference angle + ASTC sign.
